original_abstract:
The electroreduction of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is driven by metastable surface features that exhibit unusually strong CO binding. In this study, OD-Cu electrodes synthesized via the $H_2$ reduction of $Cu_2O$ precursors demonstrated high catalytic performance, converting CO to acetate and ethanol with nearly 50% Faradaic efficiency at moderate overpotentials. Using temperature-programmed desorption (TPD), we identified unique surface sites on OD-Cu that bind CO more tightly than the standard terrace and stepped sites found on polycrystalline Cu foil. 

The catalytic activity of these materials is highly sensitive to microstructure; thermal annealing at 350 °C caused a 44-fold drop in the surface-area-corrected current density and reduced the Faradaic efficiency for CO reduction to less than 5%. This deactivation directly correlates with a significant decrease in the density of the strong CO-binding sites. By integrating these findings with electron nanodiffraction mapping, we propose that the high activity and selectivity of OD-Cu stems from metastable sites supported by grain boundaries. Identifying these specific surface features represents a critical step toward tailoring the surface chemistry required for efficient electrochemical CO reduction.
humanized_abstract:
The electroreduction of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is catalyzed by metastable surface structures that have strongly-bound CO. Oxide-derived copper electrodes prepared by $H_2$ reduction of $Cu_2O$ precursors exhibit high catalytic activity for the reduction of CO to acetate and ethanol with a combined Faradaic efficiency of approximately 50% at modest overpotentials. Temperature-Programmed Desorption (TPD) experiments reveal the presence of surface structures on OD-Cu with a stronger CO binding strength than the standard terrace and stepped sites present on polycrystalline Cu foil.

The catalytic performance of OD-Cu is very sensitive to its structure. Annealing at 350 °C resulted in a 44 times decrease in the surface area normalized current and a Faradaic efficiency for CO below 5%. These observations were related to a decrease in the density of the high affinity CO binding sites on the surface, which can be understood in relation to the electron nanodiffraction mapping of the OD-Cu structure. This, together with additional experiments, points towards that the metastable sites responsible for the high selectivity and activity of OD-Cu are located at the grain boundaries. Understanding the exact surface properties of the active material is crucial for designing the right catalyst for the electrochemical reduction of CO2.

document.input:
The electroreduction of CO on oxide-derived copper (OD-Cu) is driven by metastable surface features that exhibit unusually strong CO binding. In this study, OD-Cu electrodes synthesized via the $H_2$ reduction of $Cu_2O$ precursors demonstrated high catalytic performance, converting CO to acetate and ethanol with nearly 50% Faradaic efficiency at moderate overpotentials.
